lecture_title: Stanford CS231N | Spring 2025 | Lecture 5: Image Classification with CNNs

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture begins with Justin Johnson introducing himself as the instructor for this session. He shares his academic and professional background, noting that he completed his PhD at Stanford between 2012 and 2018, where he worked on deep learning and computer vision. He also mentions his experience co-creating the CS231N course in 2015, his time at Facebook AI Research, his faculty position at the University of Michigan, and his recent involvement in a startup called World Labs.
key_takeaways: Justin Johnson is a co-creator of the CS231N course with extensive experience in deep learning and computer vision.
The lecture marks a transition point in the course, moving from deep learning basics to specialized computer vision models.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: Before diving into new material, the instructor recaps the foundational concepts covered in previous lectures. He reviews the basic image classification pipeline: transforming input pixel grids into output class scores. He discusses linear classifiers, weight matrices, and loss functions like Softmax and SVM, which measure the quality of the model's predictions. The recap also covers optimization techniques (SGD, RMSProp, Adam) for navigating the loss landscape and finding optimal weights. Notably, he mentions that the Adam optimizer recently won the 'Test of Time' award at the ICLR 2025 conference. Finally, he reviews backpropagation and computational graphs, explaining how they allow for automatic gradient computation via the chain rule, enabling the training of arbitrarily complex neural networks.
key_takeaways: Deep learning problems are formulated by mapping input tensors to output tensors and optimizing a loss function.
Computational graphs and backpropagation convert the global problem of computing gradients into a scalable local problem using the chain rule.
The Adam optimizer remains a highly influential optimization algorithm in modern machine learning.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor highlights the severe limitations of simple linear classifiers for image tasks. From a visual viewpoint, linear classifiers are constrained to learning only a single template per class. This means they struggle with intra-class variance, such as recognizing cars of different colors or shapes. From a geometric viewpoint, they can only draw linear decision boundaries, failing on datasets that are not linearly separable. While multi-layer perceptrons (fully connected neural networks) solve the linear separability issue by introducing non-linearities, they introduce a new problem: they require flattening the image into a 1D vector. This flattening operation entirely destroys the inherent 2D spatial structure of images, losing valuable geometric context.
key_takeaways: Linear classifiers are weak because they compress all knowledge of a class into a single, rigid template.
Fully connected neural networks increase representational power but destructively flatten the spatial dimensions of image inputs.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Image Features vs. End-to-End Deep Learning
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture contrasts historical computer vision techniques with modern deep learning. Before the deep learning boom, practitioners relied on manual feature extraction. Instead of feeding raw pixels into a classifier, they would write complex algorithms to extract specific properties from the image. Examples discussed include Color Histograms (which capture color distributions but discard spatial layout) and Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG, which captures local edge orientations). Often, these features were concatenated into a 'Bag of Words' representation. The instructor explains that end-to-end deep learning fundamentally shifted this paradigm. Instead of relying on human intuition to engineer features, deep learning models take raw pixels as input and use gradient descent to automatically learn the most optimal feature representations directly from the data.
key_takeaways: Historical computer vision relied heavily on human-engineered feature extractors like HOG and Color Histograms.
End-to-end deep learning replaces manual feature engineering by allowing the neural network to learn its own representations via gradient descent.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor introduces Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a specialized architecture designed to process data with a grid-like topology, such as 2D images. CNNs consist of a sequence of operators, primarily Convolution layers and Pooling layers, which preserve spatial dimensions unlike fully connected layers. The instructor briefly touches on the history of CNNs, citing Yann LeCun's LeNet in 1998 for digit recognition and the breakthrough AlexNet in 2012, which popularized CNNs due to the availability of GPUs and large datasets like ImageNet. He also notes a recent shift in the field: since 2021, Vision Transformers have begun to displace CNNs in many state-of-the-art vision tasks. Despite this, CNNs remain a foundational concept and are highly utilized in modern hybrid architectures.
key_takeaways: CNNs preserve the spatial structure of images by processing them as 3D volumes (width, height, channels).
While CNNs dominated computer vision from 2012 to 2020, Vision Transformers have become increasingly popular in recent years.

table_of_contents.topic_title: The Convolution Layer: Mechanics and Hyperparameters
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: This section deeply explores the mechanics of the Convolution layer. Instead of a weight matrix that looks at the entire image, a convolution layer uses small filters (kernels), such as a 5x5 spatial grid, that extend through the full depth (channels) of the input volume. The operation involves sliding this filter across the image and computing dot products at every spatial location. This 'template matching' process produces a 2D activation map for each filter. Stacking multiple filters results in an output volume with depth equal to the number of filters. The instructor explains key hyperparameters: Kernel Size (K) defines the spatial extent of the filter. Padding (P) involves adding zeros around the input border to prevent the output spatial dimensions from shrinking. Stride (S) defines the step size of the sliding filter; a stride greater than 1 effectively downsamples the spatial dimensions, which helps exponentially increase the receptive field in deeper layers.
key_takeaways: Convolution operates as local template matching via dot products, sliding a filter across the input image.
Padding is used to preserve spatial dimensions across layers, while Stride is used to aggressively downsample the image.
Stacking strided convolutions allows the network to rapidly build a large effective receptive field.

table_of_contents.topic_title: Convolution Layer: Example and Parameter Calculation
table_of_contents.detailed_summary: To solidify the concepts, the instructor walks through a concrete numerical example. Given an input volume of 32x32x3 and a convolutional layer with 10 filters of size 5x5, stride 1, and pad 2, he calculates the output size and number of parameters. The output spatial size is calculated using the formula: W_out = (W - K + 2P)/S + 1, yielding a 32x32x10 output volume. He then calculates the learnable parameters: each filter has 5*5*3 weights plus 1 bias, totaling 76 parameters per filter. With 10 filters, the layer has 760 parameters. He also calculates the computational cost in floating-point operations (FLOPs), showing it requires 768,000 multiply-add operations. The section concludes with visualizations of learned filters, showing that early layers naturally learn to detect basic features like oriented edges and contrasting colors.
key_takeaways: The output spatial dimension of a conv layer is determined by the formula: (W - K + 2P)/S + 1.
The number of learnable parameters in a conv layer is completely independent of the input image's spatial resolution, depending only on the filter size, input channels, and number of filters.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The instructor introduces the Pooling layer, which serves as a computationally cheap method for downsampling feature maps. Unlike convolutional layers, pooling layers contain zero learnable parameters. The most common variant is Max Pooling, where a window (e.g., 2x2) slides over the input (typically with a stride equal to the window size to avoid overlap) and extracts the maximum value in that region. This operation reduces the spatial size of the representation, making subsequent computations faster and helping to build larger receptive fields. Average pooling is another variant mentioned. Pooling operates independently on each depth slice (channel) of the input. Because Max Pooling inherently introduces a non-linearity, it can sometimes replace the need for a separate ReLU activation, though they are usually used together.
key_takeaways: Pooling layers aggressively downsample spatial dimensions without adding any learnable parameters to the model.
Max pooling operates independently on each channel and introduces a degree of spatial invariance.

table_of_contents.detailed_summary: The lecture concludes with a conceptual discussion on 'Translation Equivariance,' a vital property of Convolutional Neural Networks. The instructor explains that applying a spatial shift (translation) to an input image, and then passing it through a convolution or pooling layer, yields the exact same result as passing the original image through the layer and then shifting the resulting feature map. This mathematical property formalizes the intuition that a visual feature (like a cat's ear or an edge) should be detected exactly the same way regardless of its absolute position in the image. This inductive bias makes CNNs highly efficient and effective for visual tasks compared to fully connected networks.
key_takeaways: CNNs are translation equivariant: shifting the input shifts the output by an equivalent amount.
This property allows CNNs to reuse learned filters across the entire image, enforcing the idea that local features are location-independent.
